bdc-data-migration-008.easyUnderstanding: Simple words mein samjho: BDC development mein kuch SAP transactions hain jo developer ko daily kaam aate hain — jaise ek carpenter ke paas hammer, chisel, saw hoti hai. Inhe jaanna professional ABAP developer ki pehchaan hai. SHDB sabse pehle use karte hain — yeh transaction recorder hai. Tum SHDB open karo, transaction code likho jaise XD01, manually ek record enter karo, SAP har screen ka sequence record kar leta hai. Yeh recording hi BDC program ka blueprint ban jaata hai. SM35 session manager hai — agar tum session method use karo toh sab BDC sessions yahan dikhte hain. Yahan se sessions ko process karo, monitor karo, errors dekho, rerun karo. LSMW ek SAP tool hai jo bina coding ke migration karne deta hai — functional consultant bhi use kar sakta hai. SE38 mein custom BDC program likhte hain, SE16N se verify karte hain ki data sahi load hua ya nahi. AL11 application server ka file manager hai — jab flat file server pe upload karte hain toh yahan path check karte hain. CG3Y se file server se download karte hain, CG3Z se server pe upload karte hain — migration ke liye file transfer ke kaam aata hai. Yeh saat transactions ek BDC developer ka daily toolkit hai.
bdc-data-migration-008.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Interview mein 'BDC se related transactions batao' poochha jaaye toh interviewer test karta hai ki tum practical experience rakhhte ho ya sirf theory padhke aaye ho. Ek-ek transaction ka purpose, use case, aur connection explain karo:

SHDB (Transaction Recorder): BDC development ka starting point. Tum transaction open karo, manually ek sample record enter karo — SAP har screen ka program name, dynpro number, field name, aur value record karta hai. Yeh recording ABAP code mein convert ho jaati hai jahan hardcoded values ko file ke dynamic values se replace karte hain. SHDB use karna = field names guess karna band, exact screen structure pata rehta hai.

SM35 (Batch Input Session Manager): Session method ka dashboard. BDC_OPEN_GROUP se jo session create kiya woh yahan dikhta hai. Yahan se session ko manually ya background mein process karo. Real-time progress dekhte hain — kitne records success, kitne error. Error sessions alag dikhte hain — individual error records detail ke saath. SM35 = migration ka cockpit.

LSMW (Legacy System Migration Workbench): SAP ka no-code migration tool. Configuration-based approach — tum source file ka structure define karo, target SAP object select karo, field mapping karo. LSMW internally BDC ya Direct Input use karta hai. Functional consultants bhi LSMW use kar sakte hain without ABAP knowledge. Standard objects ke liye best choice.

SE38 (ABAP Editor): Custom BDC programs yahan likhte hain. SHDB recording ke baad generated code yahan refactor karta hai developer — file reading logic, BDCDATA population, error handling, ALV output sab SE38 mein.

SE16N (General Table Display): Migration ke baad verify karne ka tool. KNA1 mein customer create hua ya nahi, MARA mein material hai ya nahi — SE16N se instantly check karo. Count match karo source se target.

AL11 (SAP Directories): Application server ke directories yahan dikhte hain. Migration flat file server pe upload kiya — AL11 mein path verify karo. OPEN DATASET mein wohi path use karo. File exist karta hai ya nahi — AL11 se confirm karo.

CG3Y / CG3Z (File Transfer): CG3Y = server se presentation server pe download. CG3Z = presentation server se application server pe upload. Migration file laptop se server pe dena ho toh CG3Z use karo. Error log server se download karna ho toh CG3Y.
bdc-data-migration-008.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'The core transactions I use in BDC development are: SHDB for recording the screen flow of a transaction to build the BDCDATA structure — this is always the first step. SM35 for session management where I monitor batch input sessions, track progress, and analyze errors when using the session method. LSMW for standard object migration without custom coding, which I recommend to functional teams. SE38 for writing and maintaining custom Z-programs. SE16N for post-migration data verification to confirm records were created correctly. AL11 to check application server file paths for flat file uploads. CG3Y and CG3Z for transferring files between the presentation server and application server.'

Hinglish tip: Transactions ke saath unka purpose ek line mein bolna important hai — sirf list mat karo. 'SHDB se record kiya, SM35 se monitor kiya, SE16N se verify kiya' — yeh ek complete BDC workflow story hai. Interviewer ko sequence samajhni chahiye, sirf names nahi.
bdc-data-migration-008.codeExamples:
" SHDB recording se generate hue BDCDATA ko use karna — session method
DATA: lt_bdc  TYPE TABLE OF bdcdata,
      ls_bdc  TYPE bdcdata.
" BDC_OPEN_GROUP: SM35 mein visible hoga yeh session
CALL FUNCTION 'BDC_OPEN_GROUP'
  EXPORTING
    client  = sy-mandt
    groupid = 'ZMIG_VENDOR'
    user    = sy-uname
    keep    = 'X'.     " Keep session after processing for audit
" SHDB-recorded screen sequence for MK01 (Vendor Create)
ls_bdc-program = 'SAPMF02K'. ls_bdc-dynpro = '0100'. ls_bdc-dynbegin = 'X'.
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
ls_bdc-fnam = 'RF02K-LIFNR'. ls_bdc-fval = ls_vendor-lifnr.
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
ls_bdc-fnam = 'BDC_OKCODE'.  ls_bdc-fval = '/00'.
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
" BDC_INSERT: Adds this record to the SM35 session
CALL FUNCTION 'BDC_INSERT'
  EXPORTING tcode    = 'MK01'
  TABLES    dynprotab = lt_bdc.
" Close session — now visible in SM35
CALL FUNCTION 'BDC_CLOSE_GROUP'.
" Verify in SE16N: SELECT * FROM lfa1 WHERE lifnr = ls_vendor-lifnr.

SM35 for session management where I monitor batch input sessions, track progress, and analyze errors when using the session method. LSMW for standard object migration without custom coding, which I recommend to functional teams. SE38 for writing and maintaining custom Z-programs. SE16N for post-migration data verification to confirm records were created correctly. AL11 to check application server file paths for flat file uploads. CG3Y and CG3Z for transferring files between the presentation server and application server.
bdc-data-migration-008.interviewScriptHinglish: BDC development mein main jo core transactions use karta hoon woh hain — SHDB transaction ke screen flow ko record karne ke liye BDCDATA structure banane ke liye, yeh hamesha first step hota hai. SM35 session management ke liye jahan main batch input sessions monitor karta hoon, progress track karta hoon aur session method use karte time errors analyze karta hoon. wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Transaction Recorder Tcode — record karta hai screen flow (program, dynpro, field name, value) to generate BDC code skeleton.
Batch Input Session Manager Tcode — yahan BDC sessions monitor, process, debug aur error analyze karte hain.
Function Module jo naya batch input session create karta hai SM35 ke liye.
Function Module jo ek transaction (with BDCDATA) ko open session mein add karta hai.
Function Module jo session finalize karta hai — uske baad SM35 mein process karne ke liye visible ho jaata hai.
SAP standard structure (5 fields: PROGRAM, DYNPRO, DYNBEGIN, FNAM, FVAL) jo BDC ke screen flow ko represent karti hai.
Standard ABAP screen program for vendor master (MK01/XK01) — SHDB recording se milta hai.
Tcode for creating vendor master at purchasing organization level — common BDC migration target.
Reserved BDCDATA field name jo screen navigation control karta hai — values like '/00' (Enter), '=SAVE' (Save), 'BACK'.
BDC_OPEN_GROUP parameter — successfully processed sessions ko bhi SM35 mein retain karta hai for audit trail.
General Table Display Tcode — post-migration data verification ke liye target tables (LFA1, KNA1, MARA) check karte hain.

bdc-data-migration-008.quickRevisionNotes:
What: BDC ecosystem ke 7 core SAP transactions jo developer migration ke har stage pe use karta hai.

Used for:
- SHDB se transaction screens record karna aur BDC code skeleton generate karna
- SM35 se batch input sessions process karna aur per-record errors analyze karna
- LSMW se standard objects (vendor/customer/material) migrate karna without custom ABAP
- SE16N se post-migration data verification aur record count reconciliation
- AL11/CG3Z/CG3Y se application server file paths verify aur file transfer

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- SHDB (recorder), SM35 (session manager), LSMW (no-code migration), SE38 (custom programs), SE16N (table browser)
- AL11 (application server directories), CG3Z (upload to server), CG3Y (download from server)
- BDC_OPEN_GROUP, BDC_INSERT, BDC_CLOSE_GROUP — session method trio
- BDCDATA structure: PROGRAM, DYNPRO, DYNBEGIN='X', FNAM, FVAL

Interview keyword: SHDB record karo, SE38 mein code likho, CG3Z se file upload, SM35 se monitor, SE16N se verify.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is SHDB and what exactly does it record?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SHDB manual transaction execution ke dauran har screen interaction record karta hai — har screen ke liye program name, dynpro number, field technical name, aur field value capture karta hai. Yeh BDCDATA population ke liye blueprint banta hai. Main SHDB open karta hoon, new recording start karta hoon, manually ek end-to-end transaction valid test data se complete karta hoon, phir generated recording use karke ABAP BDC program banata hoon upload file ke variables se hardcoded values replace karte hue.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SHDB records every screen interaction during manual transaction execution — capturing the program name, dynpro number, field technical name, and field value for each screen. This becomes the blueprint for BDCDATA population. I open SHDB, start a new recording, manually complete one end-to-end transaction with valid test data, then use the generated recording to build the ABAP BDC program replacing hardcoded values with variables from the upload file.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: SHDB mein record karte waqt: exactly woh screens aur woh path use karo jo production mein aayega — agar koi optional popup aata hai, use bhi record karo. Nahi kiya toh production mein woh popup aane pe BDC hang karega.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Recording SHDB with a simple test case that skips optional screens — in production, those screens appear for certain data conditions and break the BDC flow.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" After SHDB recording: generated code looks like this
" PERFORM bdc_dynpro USING 'SAPMF02K' '0100'.
" PERFORM bdc_field  USING 'RF02K-LIFNR' '0000100001'. " hardcoded
" Developer replaces hardcoded values:
" PERFORM bdc_field  USING 'RF02K-LIFNR' ls_upload-lifnr. " dynamic

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use SM35 to monitor and re-process BDC sessions?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SM35 BDC session manager hai jahan main saare batch input sessions monitor karta hoon. BDC_CLOSE_GROUP ke baad, session SM35 mein appear karta hai. Wahan se main use background mein process kar sakta hoon, real-time progress monitor kar sakta hoon, aur completion ke baad statistics review kar sakta hoon — records processed, errors, time taken. Error sessions ke liye, main individual error records mein drill karta hoon exact SAP message dekhne ke liye, root cause identify karta hoon, source data correct karta hoon, aur sirf failed records resubmit karta hoon.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SM35 is the BDC session manager where I monitor all batch input sessions. After BDC_CLOSE_GROUP, the session appears in SM35. From there I can process it in background, monitor real-time progress, and after completion review the statistics — records processed, errors, time taken. For error sessions, I drill into individual error records to see the exact SAP message, identify root cause, correct the source data, and resubmit only failed records.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Large migration mein: SM35 tab open rakho doosri window mein. Har 5 minutes mein refresh karo. Error rate 0.5% se upar jaaye toh turant stop karo — root cause find karo pehle, phir aage badho.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Processing all sessions and only checking SM35 at the very end — catching errors early prevents cascading failures in dependent data objects.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SM35 session names: make them meaningful for tracking
CALL FUNCTION 'BDC_OPEN_GROUP'
  EXPORTING
    groupid = 'ZMIG_VEN_20240315'  " Date-stamped for traceability
    holddate = '20240315'           " Process not before this date
    noragent = ' '.                 " Allow agent processing

followupAnswerBank.question: When would you use LSMW instead of a custom BDC program?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Main LSMW choose karta hoon jab standard SAP objects migrate karne ho jo LSMW support karta hai — customer master, material master, vendor master, GL accounts — especially jab functional team ko ABAP involvement ke bina migration own aur run karni ho. LSMW ka configuration-based approach built-in field mapping ke saath standard objects ke liye faster set up hota hai. Main custom BDC programs likhta hoon jab target transaction LSMW se supported nahi hai, jab complex transformation logic chahiye, ya jab LSMW ki performance very large volumes ke liye insufficient ho.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I choose LSMW when migrating standard SAP objects that LSMW supports — customer master, material master, vendor master, GL accounts — especially when the functional team needs to own and run the migration without ABAP involvement. LSMW's configuration-based approach with built-in field mapping is faster to set up for standard objects. I write custom BDC programs when the target transaction is not supported by LSMW, when complex transformation logic is needed, or when LSMW's performance is insufficient for very large volumes.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project decision: 5000 material master records — used LSMW, functional team configured field mapping in 1 day, ran migration independently. Custom Z-program would have taken 3 developer-days. LSMW was the right choice here.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Building a custom BDC program for everything including standard objects where LSMW already has a tested, SAP-delivered migration object — wastes developer time unnecessarily.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" LSMW: No custom ABAP needed
" Transaction LSMW → Create Project/Subproject/Object
" Step 1: Maintain Object Attributes (choose method: BDC/Direct Input/BAPI)
" Step 2: Maintain Source Structure (define flat file columns)
" Step 3: Maintain Field Mapping (map source fields to SAP fields)
" Step 4: Specify Files → Read Data → Convert Data → Start Migration

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use AL11 and CG3Z in BDC migration workflows?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: AL11 SAP application server directory structure display karta hai — main use karta hoon yeh verify karne ke liye ki migration flat file ABAP program run karne se pehle sahi server path pe upload ho gayi hai. CG3Z presentation server (laptop) se application server pe file transfer karta hai — BDC run se pehle migration CSV upload karne ke liye use hota hai. CG3Y application server se presentation server pe download karta hai — migration program se generate hue error log files download karne ke liye use hota hai review ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: AL11 displays the SAP application server directory structure — I use it to verify that the migration flat file was uploaded to the correct server path before running the ABAP program. CG3Z transfers a file from the presentation server (laptop) to the application server — used to upload the migration CSV before the BDC run. CG3Y downloads from application server to presentation server — used to download error log files generated by the migration program for review.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Migration workflow: functional team sends CSV → developer CG3Z se server pe upload karta hai → AL11 se path confirm karta hai → ABAP program run karta hai OPEN DATASET with that path → error log generated on server → CG3Y se download karo → functional team review kare.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Hardcoding the file path in the ABAP program without checking AL11 first — different systems (DEV/QA/PROD) may have different directory structures, causing OPEN DATASET to fail.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Upload file via CG3Z to /usr/sap/trans/data/zmig_vendor.csv
" Verify in AL11: navigate to /usr/sap/trans/data/ — file should appear
OPEN DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/zmig_vendor.csv'
  FOR INPUT IN TEXT MODE ENCODING DEFAULT.
DO.
  READ DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/zmig_vendor.csv' INTO lv_line.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0. EXIT. ENDIF.
  SPLIT lv_line AT ',' INTO ls_data-lifnr ls_data-name1 ls_data-ort01.
  APPEND ls_data TO lt_vendors.
ENDDO.
CLOSE DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/zmig_vendor.csv'.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does SE16N help in validating BDC migration results?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SE16N mera primary post-migration validation tool hai. BDC run ke baad, main target master data table pe SE16N open karta hoon — vendors ke liye LFA1, customers ke liye KNA1, materials ke liye MARA — creation date ya record range se filter karta hoon, aur record count source file se expected count ke against compare karta hoon. Main 10-15 individual records ka critical field accuracy ke liye spot-check bhi karta hoon: name, address, company code assignment, aur key configuration fields. Koi bhi discrepancy functional sign-off se pehle investigation trigger karti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SE16N is my primary post-migration validation tool. After a BDC run, I open SE16N on the target master data table — LFA1 for vendors, KNA1 for customers, MARA for materials — filter by creation date or record range, and compare record count against the expected count from the source file. I also spot-check 10-15 individual records for critical field accuracy: name, address, company code assignment, and key configuration fields. Any discrepancy triggers investigation before functional sign-off.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Quick validation technique: before migration, note count of existing records in SE16N. After migration, check new count — difference should equal successfully migrated records. Cross-check with BDC error log count: (total input) - (errors) should equal (new records in SE16N).
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Only checking SE16N count without verifying actual field values — a record can be created with wrong data (truncated name, incorrect country) and count validation would pass but the data is wrong.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE16N post-validation: ABAP reconciliation query
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM lfa1
  WHERE erdat = @sy-datum    " Created today
    AND ernam = @sy-uname    " Created by migration user
  INTO @DATA(lv_created).
DATA(lv_expected) = lines( lt_input ) - lines( lt_errors ).
IF lv_created <> lv_expected.
  WRITE: / 'MISMATCH! Created:', lv_created, 'Expected:', lv_expected.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the complete BDC transaction usage sequence in a real project?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Real BDC project mein transactions ka sequence hai: screen flow record karne ke liye SHDB, custom migration program likhne aur test karne ke liye SE38, application server pe migration file upload karne ke liye CG3Z, file path verify karne ke liye AL11, program ko test mein execute karne ke liye SE38, session processing monitor karne aur errors review karne ke liye SM35, migrated records ko expected count ke against validate karne aur field accuracy ka spot-check karne ke liye SE16N, functional team review ke liye error logs download karne ke liye CG3Y. Yeh cycle final production cutover se pehle mock runs ke liye repeat hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In a real BDC project the transaction sequence is: SHDB to record the screen flow, SE38 to write and test the custom migration program, CG3Z to upload the migration file to the application server, AL11 to verify the file path, SE38 to execute the program in test, SM35 to monitor session processing and review errors, SE16N to validate migrated records against expected count and spot-check field accuracy, CG3Y to download error logs for functional team review. This cycle repeats for mock runs before the final production cutover.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Experienced developer ka tip: screenshot lo AL11 ka file path verification se pehle run, aur SE16N ka record count baad run — yeh documentation cutover sign-off ke liye useful hoti hai.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Skipping SM35 monitoring and going directly to SE16N — SM35 shows per-record errors with SAP messages which are more actionable than just a count mismatch in SE16N.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Typical migration program flow — complete skeleton
REPORT zmig_vendor_mk01.
PARAMETERS: p_file TYPE localfile DEFAULT '/usr/sap/trans/data/vendors.csv',
             p_mode TYPE c DEFAULT 'N'.  " N=prod, A=test
START-OF-SELECTION.
  PERFORM read_file    USING p_file CHANGING lt_input.
  PERFORM validate     USING lt_input CHANGING lt_errors.
  PERFORM create_session.
  LOOP AT lt_input ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>).
    PERFORM fill_bdc USING <r> CHANGING lt_bdc.
    PERFORM insert_session USING lt_bdc.
  ENDLOOP.
  PERFORM close_session.
  PERFORM display_errors USING lt_errors.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between CG3Y and CG3Z in file handling?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: CG3Z presentation server (local PC ya SAP GUI workstation) se SAP application server pe file upload karta hai — main use karta hoon BDC program run karne se pehle migration CSV file upload karne ke liye. CG3Y application server se presentation server pe download karta hai — main use karta hoon migration ke dauran server pe generate hue error log files retrieve karne ke liye, jinhe phir data correction ke liye functional team ke saath share karta hoon. Dono point-and-click GUI transactions hain jinhe koi ABAP coding nahi chahiye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: CG3Z uploads a file from the presentation server (local PC or SAP GUI workstation) to the SAP application server — I use this to upload the migration CSV file before running the BDC program. CG3Y downloads from the application server to the presentation server — I use this to retrieve error log files generated on the server during migration, which are then shared with the functional team for data correction. Both are point-and-click GUI transactions requiring no ABAP coding.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Alternative to CG3Z/CG3Y: SM36 job with program RSPO0028, or AL11 with drag-and-drop in newer SAP versions. But CG3Z/CG3Y are the classic go-to transactions that every ABAP developer knows.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Confusing the direction — trying to use CG3Z to download (it only uploads) and getting confused. Remember: CG3Z = send TO server, CG3Y = get FROM server.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" CG3Z: Upload CSV from local PC to application server
" Source: C:\migration\vendors_20240315.csv
" Target: /usr/sap/trans/data/vendors_20240315.csv

" CG3Y: Download error log from server to local PC
" Source: /usr/sap/trans/data/zmig_errors_20240315.txt
" Target: C:\migration\errors_20240315.txt

" Verify upload in AL11:
" Transaction AL11 → navigate to /usr/sap/trans/data/ → file visible

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use SE38 in the context of BDC — is there anything specific to watch for?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SE38 mein BDC-specific considerations hain: SM36 ke through background scheduling ke liye program type Executable set karo, hardcoding avoid karne ke liye selection screen pe file path aur BDC mode parameterize karo, breakpoints sirf development ke dauran MODE A ke saath use karo kyunki yeh background MODE N mein kaam nahi karte, program end mein functional team review ke liye ALV error report banao, aur production migration programs ke liye application log calls (ABAP Application Log API use karte hue) add karo taaki SM21 ya SLG1 run ke baad complete audit trail dikhaye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In SE38, BDC-specific considerations are: set program type to Executable for background scheduling via SM36, parameterize file path and BDC mode on the selection screen to avoid hardcoding, use breakpoints only with MODE A during development as they do not work in background MODE N, build an ALV error report at program end for functional team review, and for production migration programs add application log calls (using ABAP Application Log API) so that SM21 or SLG1 shows a complete audit trail after the run.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Best practice: migration program ke SE38 attributes mein 'Logical Database' blank rakhna, Logical database nahi use karna migration programs mein — direct file reading faster aur simpler hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not parameterizing the BDC mode on the selection screen — developer has to edit source code to switch between MODE A and N, which risks accidentally transporting MODE A to production.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE38: Selection screen best practice for BDC programs
REPORT zmig_cust_xd01.
PARAMETERS:
  p_file TYPE localfile OBLIGATORY,
  p_mode TYPE c DEFAULT 'N'
    AS LISTBOX VISIBLE LENGTH 1
    VALUES 'N' = 'Production (No screens)'
           'A' = 'Development (All screens)'
           'E' = 'UAT (Error screens only)'.
" Never hardcode mode — always parameterize!

bdc-data-migration-009.easyUnderstanding: Simple words mein samjho: Interview mein BDC question ke baad jo follow-up questions aate hain woh actually real project challenges hain jo interviewer test karta hai. Jaise ek doctor operation ke baad complications ke baare mein poochhe — woh jaanna chahta hai ki tum sirf basic procedure jaante ho ya complications bhi handle kar sakte ho. BDC mein teen major follow-up areas hote hain: (1) Technical challenges — popup screens handle karna, large files read karna, errors manage karna. (2) Structure samajhna — BDCDATA structure kya hai, har field ka kya kaam hai. (3) Comparison questions — BDC vs Direct Input kya fark hai, kab kaunsa use karo. Popup screens ek classic gotcha hai — 90% BDC programs fail karte hain production mein kyunki developer ne popup screen handle nahi ki. MESSAGE type 'A' matlab Abend — program wahan ruk jaata hai BDC mein. CSV file read karne ke liye GUI_UPLOAD ya OPEN DATASET use karte hain. BDCDATA structure sirf 5 fields ka simple table hai — program, dynpro, dynbegin, fnam, fval. Direct Input BDC se fast hai kyunki screen simulation nahi hoti. Yeh sab jaanne wala developer senior-level confidence dikhata hai.
bdc-data-migration-009.hinglishMasterExplanation:
BDC follow-up questions ki complete preparation karo — yeh wo questions hain jo entry-level ko filter karte hain:

Popup Screens Handle Karna: Sabse common follow-up. Production mein BDC fail karne ka number one reason hai popup screens jo SHDB recording mein capture nahi hue. Popup 2 types: (a) Informational popup — BDC_OKCODE = 'ENTER' se pass karo. (b) Confirmation popup — BDC_OKCODE = 'YES' ya specific button code se dismiss karo. Approach: SHDB recording mein intentionally popup trigger karo — woh recording capture karega. Phir BDC code mein us screen ka entry bhi add karo. Advanced: loop mein popup handle karo with conditional logic based on message type in lt_msg.

MESSAGE Type 'A' in BDC: Type 'A' matlab Abend message — yeh SAP ka hardest error hai. BDC mein jab type 'A' message aata hai toh transaction completely abort ho jaata hai, session error ho jaati hai. Cause: data validation failure at SAP level — mandatory field missing, authorization error, duplicate entry. Fix: pre-validate data before BDC, check if record already exists.

CSV File Read karna: Presentation server se GUI_UPLOAD, application server se OPEN DATASET + READ DATASET. SPLIT AT ',' statement se comma-delimited fields alag karo. Header row skip karo (first row mein column names hoti hain). Empty lines handle karo. Special characters (commas inside quoted fields) ke liye careful parsing chahiye.

BDCDATA Structure: 5 fields ka table type bdcdata. PROGRAM = screen ka ABAP program name. DYNPRO = screen number (4 digits). DYNBEGIN = 'X' sirf header row ke liye (screen start indicator). FNAM = field technical name ya 'BDC_OKCODE'. FVAL = field value ya OK code like '/00', '=SAVE', 'ENTER'.

BDC vs Direct Input: BDC screens simulate karta hai — slow but universal. Direct Input special SAP programs hain jo directly tables mein data daalta hai — fast but only for specific objects. Direct Input programs: RFBIBL00 for FI docs, RMDATIND for material master. S/4HANA mein BAPI preferred over both.
bdc-data-migration-009.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'Common follow-up areas in BDC interviews are: how to handle popup screens — I address these by recording them in SHDB with an explicit OK code entry and conditionally adding them in the BDC code based on the data scenario. Message type A in BDC means an abend that terminates the transaction — I prevent these by pre-validating mandatory fields and checking for duplicates before the BDC run. For reading CSV files, I use GUI_UPLOAD for presentation server files and OPEN DATASET with SPLIT for application server flat files. BDCDATA has five fields: PROGRAM, DYNPRO, DYNBEGIN, FNAM, and FVAL — each screen starts with a header row where DYNBEGIN is X, followed by field rows. Direct Input is faster than BDC because it bypasses screen processing — I prefer it for high-volume FI document migration using RFBIBL00.'

Hinglish tip: Follow-up answers mein real incident examples zyada effective hain. 'Popup screen ne ek baar meri production migration rok di thi — tab se har BDC recording mein deliberately popup trigger karta hoon to capture it.' Yeh maturity dikhata hai.
bdc-data-migration-009.codeExamples:
" BDCDATA structure — complete 5-field anatomy with popup handling
DATA: lt_bdc TYPE TABLE OF bdcdata,
      ls_bdc TYPE bdcdata.
" Screen header row: DYNBEGIN = 'X'
ls_bdc-program  = 'SAPMF02K'.  " Screen program name (from SHDB)
ls_bdc-dynpro   = '0100'.      " Screen number (4 digits)
ls_bdc-dynbegin = 'X'.         " 'X' = this is screen start
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
" Field row: DYNBEGIN is initial (space)
ls_bdc-fnam = 'RF02K-LIFNR'. ls_bdc-fval = ls_vendor-lifnr.
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
ls_bdc-fnam = 'BDC_OKCODE'.  ls_bdc-fval = '/00'.  " Navigate to next screen
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
" Popup screen handling — dismiss info popup
ls_bdc-program  = 'SAPLSPO1'. ls_bdc-dynpro = '0100'. ls_bdc-dynbegin = 'X'.
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
ls_bdc-fnam = 'BDC_OKCODE'. ls_bdc-fval = 'YES'.  " Confirm popup
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
CALL TRANSACTION 'MK01' USING lt_bdc MODE 'N' MESSAGES INTO DATA(lt_msg).
" Check message type A — abort case
IF line_exists( lt_msg[ msgtyp = 'A' ] ).
  " Log as hard error — do not retry
ENDIF.

wordByWordSamjho.meaning: SAP standard structure type jo BDC ke screen sequence ko hold karta hai — 5 fields ka table.
BDCDATA field — screen ka ABAP program naam (e.g. SAPMF02K for vendor master).
BDCDATA field — 4-digit screen number jaise '0100', '0110'.
BDCDATA flag — 'X' sirf screen header row mein, baaki field rows mein space (blank).
BDCDATA field — screen ka technical field name (e.g. 'RF02K-LIFNR') ya special 'BDC_OKCODE'.
BDCDATA field — actual value ya OK code (jaise '/00', '=SAVE', 'YES').
Reserved FNAM value for screen navigation — '/00' Enter, '=SAVE' Save, 'YES' popup confirm.
Standard SAP popup screen program — generic yes/no confirmation popups isi program se aate hain.
ABAP statement to execute a transaction synchronously with BDCDATA — supports MODE, UPDATE, MESSAGES INTO.
CALL TRANSACTION addition jo SAP messages (msgtyp, msgid, msgnr, msgtxt) capture karta hai for error logging.
Abend message type — transaction immediately terminate, hard failure jo retry se fix nahi hoti.
Background/no-display mode for CALL TRANSACTION — production runs ke liye standard (vs 'A' all screens, 'E' errors only).

bdc-data-migration-009.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Common BDC follow-up topics — popup screen handling, message type A meaning, CSV reading, BDCDATA structure aur BDC vs Direct Input comparison.

Used for:
- Production-grade BDC programs jo conditional popups gracefully handle karein
- Error classification — hard abend (type A) vs soft data error (type E) ko alag log karna
- Flat file reading from presentation server (GUI_UPLOAD) aur app server (OPEN DATASET)
- BDCDATA ke 5 fields ko anatomically samjhana — DYNBEGIN sirf header row pe

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- CALL TRANSACTION 'tcode' USING lt_bdc MODE 'N' MESSAGES INTO lt_msg
- SAPLSPO1 (popup screen program), BDC_OKCODE = 'YES' popup dismiss
- OPEN DATASET ... FOR INPUT IN TEXT MODE, READ DATASET, CLOSE DATASET
- SPLIT lv_line AT ',' INTO ... for CSV parsing
- Direct Input: RFBIBL00 (FI docs), RMDATIND (material master)

Interview keyword: Type 'A' = abend hard error; popup capture in SHDB; DYNBEGIN='X' only on screen header row.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle popup screens in BDC?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Popup screens BDCDATA mein explicitly handle hone chahiye — unka apna program name, dynpro number, aur unhe dismiss karne ka OK code hota hai. Main inhe SHDB recording ke dauran popup ko deliberately trigger karke capture karta hoon. Conditional popups ke liye jo sirf certain scenarios mein appear karte hain, main business rules ke based pe conditional BDCDATA entries add karta hoon, ya CALL TRANSACTION se messages check karta hoon aur agar popup-related error return hota hai toh re-process karta hoon. Common popup programs hain SAPLSPO1 generic yes/no popups ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Popup screens must be explicitly handled in BDCDATA — they have their own program name, dynpro number, and an OK code to dismiss them. I capture these by deliberately triggering the popup during SHDB recording. For conditional popups that appear only in certain scenarios, I add conditional BDCDATA entries based on business rules, or check the messages from CALL TRANSACTION and re-process if a popup-related error is returned. Common popup programs are SAPLSPO1 for generic yes/no popups.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production incident: MM01 material creation BDC — duplicate check popup appeared only when material number already existed. SHDB recording did not have it. Fixed by adding conditional BDCDATA entry: if material already exists in MARA, skip or add the popup dismiss entry.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming all executions follow the same screen flow — popups are conditional and must be tested with multiple data scenarios to ensure complete screen path coverage.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Conditional popup handling
CALL TRANSACTION 'MM01' USING lt_bdc MODE 'N' MESSAGES INTO DATA(lt_msg).
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  " Check if popup-related error — add popup dismiss and retry
  IF line_exists( lt_msg[ msgtxt CS 'duplicate' ] ).
    APPEND VALUE #( program = 'SAPLSPO1' dynpro = '0100'
                    dynbegin = 'X' ) TO lt_bdc.
    APPEND VALUE #( fnam = 'BDC_OKCODE' fval = 'YES' ) TO lt_bdc.
    CALL TRANSACTION 'MM01' USING lt_bdc MODE 'N' MESSAGES INTO lt_msg.
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is MESSAGE type A in BDC context and how do you handle it?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SAP mein Message type A matlab Abend — yeh running transaction ko bina koi partial data save kiye immediately terminate karta hai. BDC mein, type A message ka matlab woh record hard failure hai jo underlying cause fix kiye bina retry nahi ho sakta. Common reasons: missing authorization object, data constraint violation, ya required Customizing missing. Main yeh handle karta hoon lt_msg ko CALL TRANSACTION ke baad msgtyp = 'A' ke liye check karke, inhe soft errors se separately log karke, aur unhe retry ke jagah root cause investigation ke liye flag karke.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Message type A in SAP means Abend — it immediately terminates the running transaction without saving any partial data. In BDC, a type A message means that record is a hard failure that cannot be retried without fixing the underlying cause. Common reasons: missing authorization object, data constraint violation, or required Customizing missing. I handle this by checking lt_msg for msgtyp = 'A' after CALL TRANSACTION, logging these separately from soft errors, and flagging them for root cause investigation rather than retry.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Type A vs Type E: Type E is a soft error — maybe a field value is wrong, fix and retry. Type A is a hard error — missing configuration, authorization gap, or serious data issue — needs developer or basis investigation, not just data fix.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Treating all errors the same in the error log — type A and type E need different handling and different teams to fix them. Always log msgtyp separately.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
CALL TRANSACTION 'XK01' USING lt_bdc MODE 'N' MESSAGES INTO DATA(lt_msg).
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  DATA(lv_type) = VALUE #( lt_msg[ 1 ]-msgtyp OPTIONAL ).
  IF lv_type = 'A'.
    " Hard error: log for investigation, skip retry
    ls_error-error_cat = 'HARD_ABEND'.
  ELSE.
    " Soft error: log for functional data correction and retry
    ls_error-error_cat = 'DATA_ERROR'.
  ENDIF.
  APPEND ls_error TO lt_errors.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you read a CSV file for BDC in ABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Presentation server se CSV reading ke liye, main GUI_UPLOAD function module use karta hoon jo saare rows ko raw text lines ke roop mein internal table mein load karta hai — phir SPLIT AT ',' har line ko individual fields mein separate karta hai. Application server files ke liye, OPEN DATASET text mode mein followed by READ DATASET ek DO loop mein, line SPLIT karo, phir CLOSE DATASET. Main hamesha SY-TABIX ya flag variable check karke header row skip karta hoon, empty lines handle karta hoon, aur BDC logic ko pass karne se pehle validate karta hoon ki mandatory fields initial nahi hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For CSV reading from the presentation server, I use GUI_UPLOAD function module which loads all rows into an internal table as raw text lines — then SPLIT AT ',' separates each line into individual fields. For application server files, OPEN DATASET in text mode followed by READ DATASET in a DO loop, SPLIT the line, then CLOSE DATASET. I always skip the header row by checking SY-TABIX or a flag variable, handle empty lines, and validate that mandatory fields are not initial before passing to the BDC logic.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Common issue: CSV fields containing commas inside double quotes — standard SPLIT AT ',' breaks these. Solution: use a tab-delimited file instead of CSV to avoid this ambiguity. Simpler parsing and no quoting issues.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not closing the dataset after reading — CLOSE DATASET is mandatory. Omitting it causes file locks that prevent subsequent reads or writes to the same file path.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Application server CSV read for BDC
DATA: lv_line TYPE string,
      lt_input TYPE TABLE OF zsmig_vendor.
OPEN DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/vendors.csv'
  FOR INPUT IN TEXT MODE ENCODING DEFAULT.
IF sy-subrc <> 0. MESSAGE 'File not found' TYPE 'E'. RETURN. ENDIF.
DO.
  READ DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/vendors.csv' INTO lv_line.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0. EXIT. ENDIF.        " EOF
  IF sy-index = 1. CONTINUE. ENDIF.     " Skip header row
  SPLIT lv_line AT ',' INTO DATA(lv_lifnr) DATA(lv_name1) DATA(lv_ort01).
  APPEND VALUE #( lifnr = lv_lifnr  name1 = lv_name1  ort01 = lv_ort01 )
    TO lt_input.
ENDDO.
CLOSE DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/vendors.csv'.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the BDCDATA structure and explain each field?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDCDATA internal table type hai jo BDC screen simulation drive karne ke liye use hota hai. Iske paanch fields hain: PROGRAM SHDB recording se ABAP screen program name hold karta hai; DYNPRO four-digit screen number hold karta hai; DYNBEGIN 'X' set hota hai sirf screen header row ke liye naya screen start indicate karte hue; FNAM SAP screen ka technical field name hold karta hai ya 'BDC_OKCODE' navigation ke liye; FVAL field value ya OK code hold karta hai jaise Enter navigation ke liye '/00', saving ke liye '=SAVE', ya popup confirmation ke liye 'YES'. Har screen block ek header row (DYNBEGIN='X') se start hota hai followed by one or more field rows.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDCDATA is the internal table type used to drive BDC screen simulation. It has five fields: PROGRAM holds the ABAP screen program name from SHDB recording; DYNPRO holds the four-digit screen number; DYNBEGIN is set to 'X' only for the screen header row indicating a new screen start; FNAM holds the technical field name from the SAP screen or 'BDC_OKCODE' for navigation; FVAL holds the field value or an OK code like '/00' for Enter navigation, '=SAVE' for saving, or 'YES' for popup confirmation. Each screen block starts with one header row (DYNBEGIN='X') followed by one or more field rows.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Debugging tip: if BDC is failing unexpectedly, add a BREAK-POINT before CALL TRANSACTION and inspect lt_bdc in debugger — verify that PROGRAM and DYNPRO exactly match what SHDB recorded. Even one character mismatch causes SAP to not find the screen.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Setting DYNBEGIN = 'X' on field rows instead of only the header row — this causes BDC to try starting a new screen for every field entry, causing immediate failure.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BDCDATA anatomy — one complete screen example
" Row 1: Screen header (DYNBEGIN = 'X')
APPEND VALUE #( program = 'SAPMF02K' dynpro = '0100' dynbegin = 'X' )
  TO lt_bdc.
" Row 2-N: Field entries (DYNBEGIN = space, which is initial)
APPEND VALUE #( fnam = 'RF02K-LIFNR' fval = ls_data-lifnr ) TO lt_bdc.
APPEND VALUE #( fnam = 'RF02K-BUKRS' fval = '1000' )         TO lt_bdc.
APPEND VALUE #( fnam = 'BDC_OKCODE'  fval = '/00' )          TO lt_bdc.
" Row N+1: Next screen header
APPEND VALUE #( program = 'SAPMF02K' dynpro = '0110' dynbegin = 'X' )
  TO lt_bdc.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between BDC and Direct Input method?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDC SAP screen entry ko programmatically simulate karta hai — yeh har screen field exactly as a user would process karta hai, isse universal banata hai but screen processing overhead ke wajah se slower. Direct Input SAP-provided standard programs use karta hai jo screen logic bypass karte hain aur directly database tables mein write karte hain — typically 5 se 10 times faster. However, Direct Input programs sirf specific standard objects ke liye exist karte hain. Vendor master ya customer master ke liye jahan koi Direct Input program nahi hai, BDC ya BAPI required hai. Main hamesha standard SAP objects ke liye BDC choose karne se pehle Direct Input program check karta hoon.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC simulates SAP screen entry programmatically — it processes every screen field exactly as a user would, making it universal but slower due to screen processing overhead. Direct Input uses SAP-provided standard programs that bypass screen logic and write directly to database tables — typically 5 to 10 times faster. However, Direct Input programs exist only for specific standard objects. For vendor master or customer master where no Direct Input program exists, BDC or BAPI is required. I always check for a Direct Input program before choosing BDC for standard SAP objects.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: FI legacy open items migration: 200,000 documents. BDC through FB01 would take 20+ hours. RFBIBL00 Direct Input completed in 2 hours. Same data, same result — 10x speed difference. Always evaluate Direct Input first for high-volume FI migration.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not knowing that Direct Input programs exist — candidates who know only BDC and BAPI miss this option entirely. SAP Help Portal lists all available Direct Input programs per module.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Direct Input via RFBIBL00 — no custom ABAP code
" Transaction SA38 → program RFBIBL00
" Input flat file format: header record type '1', GL item type '2'
" Record type 1: BKPF fields (company code, doc date, posting date)
" Record type 2: BSEG fields (account, amount, currency)
" Submit as background job via SM36 with file path parameter
" Much faster than BDC through FB01 — recommended for FI open items

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you debug a BDC program when records are failing silently?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Silent BDC failures ke liye: pehle MESSAGES INTO lt_messages parameter add karo agar already present nahi hai — yeh exact SAP error message capture karta hai. Ek failing record ke liye MODE 'A' pe switch karo failure cause karne wali screen ko visually observe karne ke liye. Debugger mein, CALL TRANSACTION se pehle lt_bdc table inspect karo verify karne ke liye PROGRAM aur DYNPRO exactly SHDB recording se match karte hain. Session method ke liye, SM35 mein error sessions ke per-record error messages mein drill karo. Authorization failures ke liye, failed call ke turant baad SU53 run karo missing authorization object capture karne ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For silent BDC failures: first add MESSAGES INTO lt_messages parameter if not already present — this captures the exact SAP error message. Switch to MODE 'A' for a single failing record to visually observe which screen is causing the failure. In debugger, inspect the lt_bdc table before CALL TRANSACTION to verify PROGRAM and DYNPRO exactly match SHDB recording. For session method, drill into SM35 error sessions for per-record error messages. For authorization failures, run SU53 immediately after the failed call to capture the missing authorization object.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Debugging technique: run MODE 'A' with one known-bad record. Watch each screen carefully — which screen does SAP stop at? What error appears? That screen entry in BDCDATA is where the problem is. Compare field names on screen with what you have in lt_bdc — even case mismatch matters.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Debugging only in MODE N without checking messages — switch to MODE A for visual debugging, it reveals 90% of BDC issues instantly.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Debug-friendly BDC call — check every detail
CALL TRANSACTION 'MK01'
  USING lt_bdc
  MODE  'A'              " Visual mode for debugging
  UPDATE 'S'             " Synchronous update
  MESSAGES INTO DATA(lt_msg).
LOOP AT lt_msg ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<m>).
  WRITE: / <m>-msgtyp, <m>-msgid, <m>-msgnr, <m>-msgtxt.
ENDLOOP.
" Also check: is BDCDATA populated correctly?
LOOP AT lt_bdc ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<b>).
  WRITE: / <b>-program, <b>-dynpro, <b>-dynbegin, <b>-fnam, <b>-fval.
ENDLOOP.

followupAnswerBank.question: Can BDC handle table control screens (like line items in purchase orders)?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDC mein table controls ke liye per line item row ek BDCDATA block chahiye — program name aur dynpro same screen rehte hain but table control ki har row separate field entries append karke address hoti hai row-specific field names ke saath. Main 3-4 rows ke saath multi-line SHDB recording karta hoon exact field naming pattern samajhne ke liye — typically fields mein row index include hota hai jaise row 1 ke liye 'RM06E-MENGE(01)', row 2 ke liye 'RM06E-MENGE(02)'. Phir main ABAP mein LOOP banata hoon kisi bhi number of items ke liye in row-indexed field names ko dynamically generate karne ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Table controls in BDC require one BDCDATA block per line item row — the program name and dynpro remain the same screen but each row of the table control is addressed by appending separate field entries with the row-specific field names. I record a multi-line SHDB recording with 3-4 rows to understand the exact field naming pattern — typically fields include a row index like 'RM06E-MENGE(01)' for row 1, 'RM06E-MENGE(02)' for row 2. Then I build a LOOP in ABAP to dynamically generate these row-indexed field names for any number of items.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: ME21N with 10 line items: SHDB recording with 3 rows showed pattern 'RM06E-MENGE(01)', 'RM06E-MENGE(02)', 'RM06E-MENGE(03)'. ABAP loop: DO lines(lt_items) TIMES, build field name with |RM06E-MENGE({ sy-index })|. Works for any number of lines.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Recording SHDB with only one line item — you miss the multi-row field name pattern and cannot generalize to multiple items in the ABAP program.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Table control: loop through line items
DATA(lv_row) = 1.
LOOP AT lt_items ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<item>).
  " Same screen, different row index in field name
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam = |RM06E-MATNR({ lv_row })|  fval = <item>-matnr ) TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam = |RM06E-MENGE({ lv_row })|  fval = <item>-menge ) TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam = |RM06E-MEINS({ lv_row })|  fval = <item>-meins ) TO lt_bdc.
  lv_row = lv_row + 1.
ENDLOOP.

followupAnswerBank.question: What questions does an interviewer ask to test BDC depth beyond the basics?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Deep BDC interview questions include karte hain: conditional popup screens kaise handle karte hain, har BDCDATA field ka exact meaning, failed-record rerun ke liye restart strategy kaise implement karte hain, teen MODE parameter values aur kab kaunsa use karna hai, data errors se separately authorization failures kaise detect aur log karte hain, package-based processing use karke large volumes ke liye memory management, aur BDC commit control ke terms mein BAPI se kaise alag behave karta hai. Yeh questions un candidates ko separate karte hain jinhone actual BDC migrations ki hain un se jo sirf theory jaante hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Deep BDC interview questions include: how to handle conditional popup screens, the exact meaning of each BDCDATA field, how to implement a restart strategy for failed-record rerun, the three MODE parameter values and when to use each, how to detect and log authorization failures separately from data errors, memory management for large volumes using package-based processing, and how BDC behaves differently from BAPI in terms of commit control. These questions separate candidates who have done actual BDC migrations from those who know only the theory.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: From my interviews: the popup question and the 'rerun only failed records' question are asked most often. Popup: SHDB recording with popup + conditional BDCDATA. Rerun failed: use a restart table — log successful record keys, on rerun skip already-processed keys from the source.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Memorizing BDC steps without understanding why — interviewers follow up with 'why', 'what happens if you don't do this', and scenario-based questions that pure memorization cannot answer.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Restart strategy: log successful records to avoid reprocessing
SELECT lifnr FROM ztmig_done INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_done).
LOOP AT lt_source ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<s>).
  " Skip already successfully processed records
  IF line_exists( lt_done[ table_line = <s>-lifnr ] ). CONTINUE. ENDIF.
  PERFORM process_bdc USING <s>.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    INSERT ztmig_done FROM VALUE #( lifnr = <s>-lifnr ). " Mark done
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.

bdc-data-migration-010.easyUnderstanding: Simple words mein samjho: Code example se BDC samjhana matlab ek complete real-world scenario dikhana — sirf ek function nahi, pura flow. Imagine karo: ek company ka old ERP system vendor data hai — 50,000 vendors ka. Naya SAP system aa gaya. IT team ko yeh sab vendors SAP mein daalne hain. Manually enter karna impossible hai — 50,000 screens bharna hoga. BDC solution: ek ABAP program jo CSV file padhega, har vendor ka BDCDATA banayega, phir SM35 session mein daalta jaayega. Ek baar session close ho — SM35 se process karo raat bhar background mein. Classic BDC code mein teen main function calls hote hain: BDC_OPEN_GROUP (session kholna — jaise folder kholna), BDC_INSERT (har record session mein dalna — jaise file folder mein rakhna), BDC_CLOSE_GROUP (session band karna — folder seal karna). Phir SM35 se woh folder process hota hai raat ko. Error wale records alag folder mein jaate hain — functional team unhe fix karke wapas deti hai. Code example 15-20 lines mein yeh complete flow dikhata hai — interviewer ko samajh aata hai ki tum practical mein kaam kar sakte ho.
bdc-data-migration-010.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Code example ke saath BDC explain karna interview ka strongest play hai — yeh pure theory answer se 10x better hota hai. Session method ka complete flow explain karo code ke saath:

Phase 1 — Setup: BDC_OPEN_GROUP function module call karo. Parameters: CLIENT (system client), GROUPID (unique session naam — meaningful rakhna jaise 'ZMIG_VENDOR_001'), USER (kisne create kiya), KEEP ('X' matlab processed session bhi rakhna for audit), HOLDDATE (kab process karna allow hai). Yeh call ek BDC session create karta hai jo SM35 mein visible hota hai.

Phase 2 — BDCDATA populate karo har record ke liye: SHDB se liya hua screen structure use karo. Vendor create ke liye MK01 transaction — pehle screen 0100 (vendor number + company code), phir screen 0110 (general data), phir screen save. Har screen ke liye: pehle header row (program, dynpro, dynbegin='X'), phir field rows (fnam, fval). File se data padhke dynamic values daalo.

Phase 3 — BDC_INSERT: Har vendor ke BDCDATA ko session mein insert karo. Parameters: TCODE (transaction code), DYNPROTAB (lt_bdc table). Yeh ek record ko session queue mein add karta hai — abhi process nahi hota.

Phase 4 — BDC_CLOSE_GROUP: Session finalize karo. Ab SM35 mein session dikhega — process karo manually ya background job se.

Error reading: BDC_CLOSE_GROUP ke baad SM35 se ya program ke end mein statistics function se error rate check karo. Individual error records ke liye SM35 mein drill down karo — message type A/E, field name, screen number sab dikhta hai.

Complete flow in ~20 lines of meaningful ABAP code — yeh interview mein code whiteboard pe likhne ka confidence dikhata hai.
bdc-data-migration-010.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'Let me walk you through a complete BDC session method example for vendor master migration using MK01. First, BDC_OPEN_GROUP creates the batch session — I give it a meaningful name like ZMIG_VENDOR with KEEP = X for audit trail. Then for each vendor record from the flat file, I populate the BDCDATA table based on the SHDB recording — screen 0100 for vendor number and company code, screen 0110 for general data fields like name and address, finishing with a save OK code. BDC_INSERT adds each vendor's BDCDATA to the session without processing it yet. Finally BDC_CLOSE_GROUP seals the session — it then appears in SM35 where it can be processed in background. Error records are automatically separated in SM35 with the exact SAP error message per record. This approach handled our 50,000 vendor migration with less than 0.5% errors.'

Hinglish tip: Code example bolte waqt structure describe karo, sab lines recite mat karo. 'Pehle session open, phir har vendor ka BDCDATA banao loop mein, insert karo session mein, phir close karo — SM35 rest handle karta hai.' Yeh summary effective hai, phir details pooche jaane pe code explain karo.
bdc-data-migration-010.codeExamples:
" Complete BDC Session Method — MK01 Vendor Master Migration
REPORT zmig_vendor_mk01.
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_vendor,
  lifnr TYPE lfa1-lifnr,  name1 TYPE lfa1-name1,
  ort01 TYPE lfa1-ort01,  land1 TYPE lfa1-land1,
END OF ty_vendor.
DATA: lt_vendors TYPE TABLE OF ty_vendor,
      lt_bdc     TYPE TABLE OF bdcdata.
" Step 1: Open session — visible in SM35
CALL FUNCTION 'BDC_OPEN_GROUP'
  EXPORTING client  = sy-mandt  groupid = 'ZMIG_VEN'
             user   = sy-uname  keep    = 'X'.
" Step 2: Read flat file (app server)
OPEN DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/vendors.csv' FOR INPUT IN TEXT MODE
  ENCODING DEFAULT.
DO.
  DATA(lv_line) = VALUE string( ).
  READ DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/vendors.csv' INTO lv_line.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0. EXIT. ENDIF.
  IF sy-index = 1. CONTINUE. ENDIF.          " Skip header
  SPLIT lv_line AT ',' INTO DATA(lv_lifnr) DATA(lv_name) DATA(lv_city) DATA(lv_ctry).
  " Step 3: Build BDCDATA from SHDB recording
  CLEAR lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( program='SAPMF02K' dynpro='0100' dynbegin='X' ) TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam='RF02K-LIFNR' fval=lv_lifnr )              TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam='RF02K-BUKRS' fval='1000' )                TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam='BDC_OKCODE'  fval='/00' )                 TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( program='SAPMF02K' dynpro='0110' dynbegin='X' ) TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam='LFA1-NAME1'  fval=lv_name )               TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam='LFA1-ORT01'  fval=lv_city )               TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam='LFA1-LAND1'  fval=lv_ctry )               TO lt_bdc.
  APPEND VALUE #( fnam='BDC_OKCODE'  fval='=SAVE' )               TO lt_bdc.
  " Step 4: Insert this vendor into session
  CALL FUNCTION 'BDC_INSERT'
    EXPORTING tcode = 'MK01'  TABLES dynprotab = lt_bdc.
ENDDO.
CLOSE DATASET '/usr/sap/trans/data/vendors.csv'.
" Step 5: Close session — process via SM35
CALL FUNCTION 'BDC_CLOSE_GROUP'.
WRITE: / 'Session ZMIG_VEN created. Process via SM35.'.
